attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll run the Refactor phase for the claim-office implementation. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md
# Architecture Notes

## After cycle 1
- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.

## After cycle 2
- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).

## After cycle 3
- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).

## After cycle 4
- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.

## After cycle 5
- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.

## After cycle 6
- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).

## After cycle 7
- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).

content.text:
All 8 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities.

## Refactor Phase Evaluation — Cycle 8 (Loyalty Multiplier)

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

**Current names in the cycle-8 addition:**
- `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER` (module constant, 0.8) — does it reveal intent? The spec calls this the "loyal customer discount" applied when `yearsWithMHPCO >= 2`. Name is accurate; matches naming convention of other `*_MULTIPLIER` constants.
- `loyaltyMultiplier` (local binding) — mirrors module constant, matches the convention from cycle 5 (`cursedMultiplier`).
- `runScenario`, `itemPrice`, `itemModifierMultiplier`, `ceilInMhpcoFavor`, `customer`, `steps`, `item` — all unchanged from prior cycles, already evaluated.

**Decision:** Keep current names. Naming is consistent with established convention.

### Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass (focusing on the cycle-8 delta in `runScenario`)

```
runScenario body:
  const { customer, steps } = scenario as Scenario;                  // bindings: 2, invocations: 0
  const item = steps[0].items[0];                                    // bindings: 1, constants: 2 (0, 0), invocations: 2 (2 indexes)
  const itemPrice = BASE_PRICE[item.type] * itemModifierMultiplier(item); // bindings: 1, invocations: 3 (index, call, *)
  const loyaltyMultiplier =
    customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1;           // bindings: 1, conditionals: 1, constants: 2 (2, 1), invocations: 1 (>=)
  const premium =
    ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * loyaltyMultiplier * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE; // bindings: 1, invocations: 4 (ceilInMhpcoFavor, *, *, +)
  return { results: [{ premium }] };                                  // 1 invocation (object/array construction trivial)

Approx mass:
- Constants: 4
- Bindings: ~10 (incl. params + module refs)
- Invocations: ~11 × 2 = 22
- Conditionals: 1 × 4 = 4
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0
Total ≈ ~41
```

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 8 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The loyalty ternary is inline at the use-site. Two candidates:
- (a) Extract `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` helper, paralleling `itemModifierMultiplier(item)`. This would create a symmetric pricing pipeline: item-side modifiers + customer-side modifiers, both as named helpers.
- (b) Leave inline — only one customer-side modifier exists.

The cycle-2 history note flagged the "post-first contract discount" (0.85) as a future customer-side multiplier (see cycle-2 test list, last quote test). That's a **second** customer-side modifier coming. By cycle-5's own rule ("only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet — Rule 3 needs a driver"), I should NOT extract yet. Extracting would be speculative.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication exists. The cycle-2 prediction about post-first-contract-discount is a forecast, not duplication present today.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Premature helper would add a function frame for a single ternary, raising mass without justification.

### Step 4: Naming Drift Check on `itemModifierMultiplier`

The cycle-6 note established that `itemModifierMultiplier` handles **item-attribute** multipliers (cursed, enchantment), distinct from customer-side multipliers (loyalty, first-insurance). The cycle-8 addition (loyalty) sits at the `runScenario` level, NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`. Good — the helper's name and scope are still accurate.

### Step 5: Symmetry Improvement Consideration

Looking at the premium line: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * loyaltyMultiplier * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.

The first-insurance loading (1.10) is also a **customer-side** modifier — it depends on whether the customer has prior contracts (currently always true for these tests, i.e., always applied because there's no "subsequent insurance" branch yet). Cycle-2's todo lists "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 56 (60 × 0.85 + 5, post-first contract discount)" — that's the test that will replace the constant 1.10 with conditional logic (first quote: 1.10; subsequent: 0.85).

That future test is the real driver for extracting a `customerLoadingMultiplier(customer, quoteIndex)` helper. Extracting now without that driver would lock in the wrong shape (it would have customer as the only parameter, then need reworking when quote index enters).

### Decision: No Structural Refactoring This Cycle

The cycle-8 change introduced one ternary at the appropriate level (scenario body, not item-helper). All naming follows established conventions. Extracting a `customerDiscountMultiplier` helper is speculative — Rule 4 says wait for the driver. The driver (second customer-side modifier — the 0.85 post-first-contract discount) is on the test list and will land in a few cycles, at which point extracting becomes natural.

### Step 6: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 8
- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
EOF
echo "Appended"

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

---

**Refactor Phase Summary — Cycle 8 (Loyalty Multiplier)**

**Naming evaluation (first priority):**
- `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER` / `loyaltyMultiplier` — accurate, follows the cycle-5 convention (full word `Multiplier`, mirrors module constant)
- `itemModifierMultiplier` — still accurate; loyalty is correctly placed at `runScenario` scope, not inside this item-attribute helper (preserving cycle-6 scoping)
- All other names unchanged from prior cycles

**Refactoring applied:** None.

**Justification (documented in `architecture-notes.md`):**
- Rule 1: 8/8 tests green
- Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming is consistent with established conventions; no clarity gain available
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so no duplication driver
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` now would be speculative and would lock in the wrong signature — the upcoming "post-first-contract 0.85 discount" test (cycle-2 forecast on the test list) introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency, so the natural extraction point is then, not now

**APP mass:** ~41 (unchanged from cycle 7's structure; loyalty ternary added ~5 mass for the new conditional + binding)

**Tests:** All 8 passing.

**Files updated:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md` (appended cycle-8 entry)

Proceeding to the next test.
